```{r proximity-comparison}
cat("\nCountry proximity matrices:\n")
cat("  Spearman rank correlation:", round(cor_country_prox, 3), "\n")
```

```{r proximity-scatter, fig.height=4.5}
# Scatter: co-location vs Spearman proximity (features)
prox_compare_df <- tibble(
  colocation = coloc_upper,
  spearman = spear_upper
)

p1 <- ggplot(prox_compare_df, aes(x = colocation, y = spearman)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1, size = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = highlight, se = FALSE) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = gl$text_muted) +
  labs(x = "Co-location proximity", y = "Spearman proximity (rescaled)")

# Scatter: co-location vs Spearman proximity (countries)
prox_compare_cc_df <- tibble(
  colocation = coloc_cc_upper,
  spearman = spear_cc_upper
)

p2 <- ggplot(prox_compare_cc_df, aes(x = colocation, y = spearman)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.1, size = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = highlight, se = FALSE) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = gl$text_muted) +
  labs(x = "Co-location proximity", y = "Spearman proximity (rescaled)")

p1 + p2 + plot_annotation(tag_levels = "A")

save_fig("full", "proximity_comparison_scatter.pdf")
```

## Comparing embeddings

Do the two proximity methods produce similar institution spaces? We compare by computing
the Spearman correlation between pairwise distances in each UMAP embedding.

```{r embedding-comparison}
# Pairwise Euclidean distances in each institution UMAP
dist_coloc <- as.matrix(dist(umap_inst[, 1:2]))
dist_spear <- as.matrix(dist(umap_inst_sp[, 1:2]))

# Correlation between pairwise distances (Mantel-like test)
d_coloc_upper <- dist_coloc[upper.tri(dist_coloc)]
d_spear_upper <- dist_spear[upper.tri(dist_spear)]

cat("Institution UMAP distance correlation (Spearman):",
    round(cor(d_coloc_upper, d_spear_upper, method = "spearman"), 3), "\n")

# Same for country UMAP
dist_coloc_c <- as.matrix(dist(umap_country[, 1:2]))
dist_spear_c <- as.matrix(dist(umap_country_sp[, 1:2]))

d_coloc_c_upper <- dist_coloc_c[upper.tri(dist_coloc_c)]
d_spear_c_upper <- dist_spear_c[upper.tri(dist_spear_c)]

cat("Country UMAP distance correlation (Spearman):",
    round(cor(d_coloc_c_upper, d_spear_c_upper, method = "spearman"), 3), "\n")
```

## ECI vs PCA comparison

The eigenvalue-based ECI extracts a single dimension of complexity from the co-location
structure. PCA extracts dimensions of variance from the raw data. How do they relate?

```{r eci-vs-pca}
compare_df <- tibble(
  country = rownames(Mcp),
  ICI = eci_eigen,
  PC1 = pca_result$x[, 1],
  PC2 = pca_result$x[, 2],
  diversity = kc0
) %>%
  mutate(
    region = region_labels[as.character(region_lookup[country])],
    region = replace_na(region, "Other")
  )

ggplot(compare_df, aes(x = ICI, y = PC1)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = region), size = 2.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = gl$text_muted, linetype = "dashed", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_text_repel(aes(label = country), size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 15) +
  labs(x = "ICI (eigenvalue method)", y = "PC1 score")

save_fig("full", "eci_vs_pca.pdf")

cat("Correlation ICI vs PC1:", round(cor(compare_df$ICI, compare_df$PC1), 3), "\n")
cat("Correlation ICI vs PC2:", round(cor(compare_df$ICI, compare_df$PC2), 3), "\n")
```

# Additional Visualizations

## Heatmap of the Mcp Matrix

```{r heatmap, fig.width=6.5, fig.height=6.5}
# Order columns by ubiquity (most ubiquitous = left) and rows by diversity (most diverse = top)
col_order <- names(sort(kp0, decreasing = TRUE))
row_order <- names(sort(kc0, decreasing = TRUE))

Mcp_ordered <- Mcp[row_order, col_order]

pheatmap(
  Mcp_ordered,
  color = colorRampPalette(c("grey95", gl$brand_blue))(100),
  show_rownames = FALSE,
  show_colnames = FALSE,
  cluster_rows = FALSE,
  cluster_cols = FALSE,
  fontsize = 8
)
```

## ICI vs GDP per Capita

```{r gdp-data, cache=TRUE}
# Get GDP per capita from World Development Indicators
gdp_data <- WDI(
  indicator = "NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD",
  start = latest_year - 1,
  end = latest_year,
  extra = TRUE
) %>%
  filter(!is.na(NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD)) %>%
  group_by(country) %>%
  slice_max(year, n = 1) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(country, iso3c, gdp_pc = NY.GDP.PCAP.PP.KD)

# Match to our country names using countrycode
eci_gdp <- eci_df %>%
  mutate(
    iso3c = countrycode(country, "country.name", "iso3c",
                        warn = FALSE)
  ) %>%
  left_join(gdp_data, by = "iso3c") %>%
  filter(!is.na(gdp_pc))

cat("Countries matched with GDP data:", nrow(eci_gdp), "\n")
```

```{r eci-vs-gdp}
# Add regions
eci_gdp <- eci_gdp %>%
  mutate(
    region = region_labels[as.character(
      region_lookup[country.x]
    )],
    region = replace_na(region, "Other")
  )

cor_gdp <- cor(eci_gdp$ICI, log(eci_gdp$gdp_pc), use = "complete.obs")

ggplot(eci_gdp, aes(x = ICI, y = gdp_pc)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = region), size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = gl$text_muted, linetype = "dashed", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country.x),
    size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 15
  ) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::dollar_format()) +
  labs(
    x = "Institutional Complexity Index (ICI)",
    y = "GDP per capita (PPP, constant $, log scale)"
  )

save_fig("full", "ici_vs_gdp.pdf")
```

## ICI vs Liberal Democracy Index

```{r eci-vs-libdem}
# Get liberal democracy index
libdem <- vdem_raw %>%
  filter(year == latest_year) %>%
  select(country_name, v2x_libdem) %>%
  filter(!is.na(v2x_libdem))

eci_libdem <- eci_df %>%
  left_join(libdem, by = c("country" = "country_name")) %>%
  filter(!is.na(v2x_libdem)) %>%
  mutate(
    region = region_labels[as.character(region_lookup[country])],
    region = replace_na(region, "Other")
  )

cor_libdem <- cor(eci_libdem$ICI, log(eci_libdem$v2x_libdem), use = "complete.obs")

ggplot(eci_libdem, aes(x = ICI, y = v2x_libdem)) +
  geom_point(aes(color = region), size = 3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = gl$text_muted, linetype = "dashed", se = TRUE, alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_text_repel(
    aes(label = country),
    size = 2.5, max.overlaps = 15
  ) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    x = "Institutional Complexity Index (ICI)",
    y = "V-Dem Liberal Democracy Index (log scale)"
  )

save_fig("full", "ici_vs_libdem.pdf")
```

## ICI by World Region

```{r eci-by-region}
eci_regions <- eci_df %>%
  mutate(
    region = region_labels[as.character(region_lookup[country])],
    region = replace_na(region, "Other")
  ) %>%
  filter(region != "Other")

ggplot(eci_regions, aes(x = reorder(region, ICI, median), y = ICI, fill = region)) +
  geom_boxplot(alpha = 0.7, show.legend = FALSE, outlier.alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_jitter(aes(color = region), width = 0.15, alpha = 0.5, size = 1.5, show.legend = FALSE) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Institutional Complexity Index (ICI)")

save_fig("full", "ici_by_region.pdf")
```

# Discussion

## What does institutional complexity tell us?

The analogy between economic and institutional complexity reveals several patterns:

**High-complexity countries** tend to be established democracies with deep, interlocking
institutional frameworks. These countries don't just have individual strong institutions --
they have *rare combinations* of institutions that reinforce each other. Just as economically
complex countries produce products that require many complementary capabilities, institutionally
complex countries maintain governance features that require deep state capacity, civic culture,
and historical development.

**The institution space** reveals how governance features cluster. Features that are close
together in the UMAP embedding tend to co-occur -- they represent institutional "packages"
that countries adopt together. The structure of this space suggests that some institutional
reforms may be easier to adopt once neighboring capabilities are in place.

**Feature complexity** is particularly informative. The most "complex" institutional features --
those found primarily in institutionally sophisticated countries -- tend to involve subtle
aspects of governance: rigorous and impartial public administration, judicial accountability,
freedom from self-censorship in media, and deliberative quality of political discourse.
These are features that require not just formal rules but deeply embedded norms and practices.

## Methodological notes

This analysis uses a **continuous Mcp matrix** (min-max normalized to [0, 1]) rather than
the traditional binary matrix. This preserves the full variation in V-Dem's IRT-derived
scores and avoids arbitrary binarization thresholds. Two discrete integer variables
(v2regopploc, v2regsuploc) were excluded since they do not sit on comparable continuous
scales.

We compute proximity using two methods:

1. **Co-location proximity** (EC-style): the continuous generalization of the Hidalgo et al.
   minimum conditional probability. Captures whether high values tend to co-occur.

2. **Spearman rank correlation**: captures the full monotonic relationship between indicators
   (or countries), including agreement at the bottom of the distribution. Rescaled to [0, 1]
   for comparability.

The comparison between these methods tests whether the institution space structure is
driven primarily by which countries score *high* on features (co-location) or by the full
rank ordering (Spearman). PCA provides a multi-dimensional view complementary to the single
ECI axis.

## Caveats

1. **V-Dem data reflects expert assessments**, not objective measures. The normalization
   choices (min-max vs z-score vs rank) affect results.

2. **The analogy has limits.** Countries don't "choose" institutions the way firms choose
   products to export. Institutional development is path-dependent, often violent, and
   constrained by geography, history, and international power relations.

3. **Correlation with GDP and democracy is expected** given that many V-Dem indicators
   directly measure democratic quality. The interesting question is whether the *complexity*
   measure adds information beyond simple additive indices.

4. **The method is sensitive to indicator selection.** A different set of indicators would
   produce different complexity rankings. Our selection, while broad, reflects choices about
   what counts as an "institution."

5. **Cross-sectional analysis only.** A natural extension would track institutional complexity
   over time to study how countries build (or lose) institutional sophistication.

## Future directions

- Panel analysis: how does institutional complexity evolve over time?
- Predictive power: does ICI predict future economic growth or democratic stability
  beyond what simpler indices capture?
- Institutional "product space" analysis: which institutional upgrades are most
  feasible for countries at different positions in the institution space?
- Comparison with actual Economic Complexity Index: are economically and institutionally
  complex countries the same?
- Alternative normalizations: rank-based or z-score normalization instead of min-max

---

*Analysis generated on `r Sys.Date()`. Data from V-Dem (loaded from `data/shared-data/vdem/`)
and World Development Indicators.*
